subsystem: A Node.js native (C++) addon method, DetectCharacterEncoding, that wraps ICU's charset-detection API (ucsdet_*). It takes a JavaScript Buffer from the caller, feeds its raw bytes to an ICU UCharsetDetector, runs detection, and returns a JS object { encoding, confidence } describing the guessed character encoding. It is the boundary layer between untrusted JavaScript-supplied byte buffers and the ICU C library.
provenance: Identified from the code itself: the NAN_METHOD(...) macro, Nan::HandleScope, Nan::ThrowError, Nan::New<v8::...>, and info.GetReturnValue().Set are the Native Abstractions for Node.js (NAN) / V8 embedding API; node::Buffer::Data / node::Buffer::Length are Node.js buffer accessors; ucsdet_open / ucsdet_setText / ucsdet_detect / ucsdet_getName / ucsdet_getConfidence / ucsdet_close plus UCharsetDetector, UCharsetMatch, UErrorCode, U_ZERO_ERROR and U_FAILURE are ICU's charset-detector C API (unicode/ucsdet.h). The leading comment '/* sample: primevul_train_2118_vuln */' and the filename eval_0067_cwe755.cpp associate it with the node-icu-charset-detector addon; CWE-755 = improper handling of exceptional conditions.
trust_boundary: The trust boundary is the JavaScript-to-native call. Everything in info[] is attacker/caller-controlled: info[0] may be absent (undefined), the wrong type (not an Object, not a Buffer), or an empty/zero-length Buffer, and the buffer's bytes are arbitrary. The native code must treat info[0] and its contents as hostile. ICU's return values (errorCode AND the returned pointers charsetMatch / charsetName) are the results of parsing that hostile input and must each be validated before use; in particular ICU may report success via errorCode yet still return a NULL match for input it cannot classify.

what_the_code_does.explanation: NAN_METHOD(DetectCharacterEncoding) declares a function callable from JavaScript through the NAN binding layer; NAN expands it to a function receiving a Nan::FunctionCallbackInfo named 'info' that holds the JS arguments (info[0], info[1], ...), the receiver, and the return-value slot. Nan::HandleScope scope establishes a V8 handle scope so that any v8::Local handles created below are properly tracked and released when the function returns. This is the standard entry-point boilerplate for a native method exposed to Node.js.

The first JavaScript argument is read as info[0] and coerced to a v8::Object via the legacy ToObject() overload, storing the result in inputBuffer. The intent is that the caller passes a Node.js Buffer (which is a JS object) whose backing bytes will be detected. Note that there is no check that any argument was passed (info.Length()), no check that info[0] is an Object, and no check that it is actually a Buffer — the code trusts the caller to have supplied a Buffer here.

Three locals are declared: charsetDetector (an opaque ICU detector handle to be created below), charsetMatch (a const pointer to an ICU match object that ucsdet_detect will return), and errorCode initialized to U_ZERO_ERROR (ICU's 'no error' sentinel). ICU uses the in/out UErrorCode convention: callers pass &errorCode into each API, and each API is expected to leave it unchanged on success or set a failure code on error. charsetDetector and charsetMatch are left uninitialized and only assigned later.

ucsdet_open(&errorCode) allocates a new ICU charset detector and returns its handle, reporting any failure through errorCode. The U_FAILURE(errorCode) macro is true for ICU error codes; if allocation failed, the code throws a JS error and returns. Note this early return does NOT call ucsdet_close, which is acceptable only because on failure ucsdet_open does not hand back a valid detector to close.

ucsdet_setText feeds the input bytes into the detector: node::Buffer::Data(inputBuffer) returns a char* to the buffer's raw memory and node::Buffer::Length(inputBuffer) returns its byte length; these become the text and textLength arguments. This is where attacker-controlled bytes enter ICU. The errorCode is checked afterward; on failure it throws, closes the detector to avoid a leak, and returns. Both node::Buffer::Data and node::Buffer::Length assume inputBuffer is a real Buffer object; they are not validated here.

ucsdet_detect runs the actual heuristic detection over the previously set text and returns a pointer to the best UCharsetMatch, again reporting through errorCode. The code checks ONLY errorCode via U_FAILURE, then throws/closes/returns on error. Crucially it does NOT check whether the returned charsetMatch pointer is NULL — ICU's ucsdet_detect can legitimately return NULL (e.g. for empty input or when no encoding can be determined) while leaving errorCode as U_ZERO_ERROR, so a NULL match survives this gate.

ucsdet_getName(charsetMatch, &errorCode) dereferences the match object to obtain the encoding name string (e.g. "UTF-8"). It takes charsetMatch by value but internally reads through it. Because the previous step never confirmed charsetMatch is non-NULL, this call is the point where a NULL match is dereferenced. The subsequent U_FAILURE check inspects errorCode again and handles ICU-reported failures, but a NULL-pointer dereference is a crash, not an ICU error code.

ucsdet_getConfidence(charsetMatch, &errorCode) reads the detector's confidence score (0-100) for the match into an int32_t. Like getName it dereferences charsetMatch and thus shares the same NULL-match hazard. The errorCode is checked and the same throw/close/return cleanup pattern is applied on ICU-reported failure.

A new JS object is created and populated with two properties: 'encoding' set to a v8::String built from charsetName, and 'confidence' set to a v8::Number built from the confidence int. Nan::New<v8::String>(charsetName) constructs a V8 string from the C string; .ToLocalChecked() asserts the MaybeLocal is non-empty and will abort/crash if it is empty. info.GetReturnValue().Set(obj) returns the object to JavaScript, and ucsdet_close(charsetDetector) frees the ICU detector on the success path. This is the only success-path cleanup of the detector.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: This is the primary vulnerability and matches the CWE-755 (improper handling of an exceptional condition) framing of the sample. ICU's ucsdet_detect can return a NULL UCharsetMatch* while leaving errorCode == U_ZERO_ERROR — this happens for input the detector cannot classify, most reliably an empty (zero-length) Buffer, but also certain short/ambiguous inputs. The code only inspects errorCode with U_FAILURE and never checks 'if (charsetMatch == NULL)'. A NULL match therefore passes the gate at lines 33-37 and is handed to ucsdet_getName(charsetMatch, ...) at line 39 (and ucsdet_getConfidence at line 47), which dereference it. An attacker who can reach this method with an empty Buffer (e.g. Buffer.alloc(0)) triggers a NULL-pointer dereference that crashes the Node.js process — a remotely reachable denial of service if this method sits behind any request path. The invariant the implementation must uphold: after ucsdet_detect, treat a NULL return as a failure (throw + close + return) BEFORE calling any ucsdet_get* on the match, regardless of errorCode.

There is no argument-count or type validation before this line. If the method is invoked with no arguments, info[0] is Undefined; calling ->ToObject() on Undefined/Null yields an empty MaybeLocal/handle in modern V8 and throws a TypeError or produces an empty v8::Local. Assigning that empty handle to inputBuffer and later passing it to node::Buffer::Data / node::Buffer::Length leads to dereferencing an empty/invalid handle or reading a non-Buffer object as a Buffer. Even when info[0] is a non-empty object that is NOT a Buffer, node::Buffer::Data(inputBuffer) returns garbage/asserts and node::Buffer::Length returns an arbitrary value, so ICU is fed a bogus pointer/length pair. The implementation must gate on info.Length() >= 1 and node::Buffer::HasInstance(info[0]) (throwing a clean JS error otherwise) before touching the buffer. This is missing input validation feeding a possible crash.

This is the concrete consequence of the missing validation above: node::Buffer::Data(inputBuffer) and node::Buffer::Length(inputBuffer) assume inputBuffer is a genuine Node Buffer. If it is not (wrong type, or an empty handle from a coercion failure at line 5), Data() may return NULL or an unrelated pointer while Length() returns an unrelated integer, and ICU then reads that many bytes from an invalid pointer — an out-of-bounds read / crash driven entirely by caller input. The safe contract is that this line is only reached after confirming a real Buffer, so that the (pointer, length) pair genuinely describes the buffer's memory.

charsetName comes from ucsdet_getName and, on any path where it is NULL (for example if the NULL-match bug above were only partially fixed by checking errorCode but not the pointer, or if ICU returns a NULL name without an error), Nan::New<v8::String>(charsetName) is constructed from a NULL char*. Building a V8 string from NULL yields an empty MaybeLocal, and the immediately chained .ToLocalChecked() asserts non-emptiness and will abort the process (a hard crash) when the MaybeLocal is empty. The implementation should verify charsetName != NULL (and use a non-aborting conversion) before constructing the result object.

summary: This native Node.js addon feeds an attacker-controlled Buffer into ICU charset detection but mishandles two exceptional conditions: ucsdet_detect can return a NULL match while reporting no error (only errorCode is checked), so the following ucsdet_getName/ucsdet_getConfidence dereference NULL and crash the process on empty/ambiguous input; and info[0] is never validated as a real Buffer before use. The dominant risk is a remotely triggerable NULL-pointer-dereference denial of service (CWE-755/CWE-476).

reviewed_anchors.reason: Entry-point signature/boilerplate; the attack surface is the info[] arguments it exposes, reviewed separately.
No argument-count or Buffer type check on attacker-controlled info[0] before coercion and use.
Local handle; failure of ucsdet_open is checked at 13-16 and the success/error paths close it; no leak on the guarded early return.
Return of ucsdet_detect used at 39/47 without a NULL check; core defect.
Correctly initialized and checked after every ICU call; the bug is that errorCode alone is insufficient (pointer nullness unchecked), captured in the charsetMatch finding.
Return checked via errorCode; early return without close is safe because open returns no valid handle on failure.
Data()/Length() on an unvalidated object can produce a bogus pointer/length pair passed to ICU (OOB read).
Can return NULL with errorCode==U_ZERO_ERROR; only errorCode is checked.
Dereferences possibly-NULL charsetMatch; also charsetName may be NULL feeding line 56.
Same NULL-match root cause as the charsetMatch finding; not a materially distinct defect.
ToLocalChecked() aborts if charsetName is NULL/empty-string construction fails.
Detector is closed on every error path and on the success path; no resource leak or double-close observed in the shown code.
Standard return of a fully-populated object; no security contract of its own.

findings.title: NULL-match dereference: ucsdet_detect return not checked for NULL (only errorCode)
findings.bug_class: NULL pointer dereference from improperly handled exceptional condition
findings.invariant: After ucsdet_detect, the returned UCharsetMatch* must be checked for NULL and treated as a failure (throw, close the detector, return) BEFORE any ucsdet_getName/ucsdet_getConfidence call, independently of errorCode.
findings.failure_mode: ICU's ucsdet_detect returns NULL when it cannot determine an encoding (reliably reproducible with an empty/zero-length Buffer, and possible for short ambiguous input) while leaving errorCode == U_ZERO_ERROR, so the U_FAILURE gate passes. ucsdet_getName(charsetMatch, ...) at line 39 (and ucsdet_getConfidence at line 47) then dereference the NULL match, crashing the Node.js process. If this method is reachable from untrusted request data, it is a remote denial of service.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the implementation has no 'if (charsetMatch == NULL)' (or equivalent) guard between lines 31 and 39, and confirm empirically that ucsdet_detect returns NULL for a zero-length Buffer with errorCode unset.

findings.title: Missing argument/type validation on info[0] before Buffer access
findings.bug_class: Missing input validation (untrusted argument used without type/arity check)
findings.invariant: Before coercing/using info[0], verify info.Length() >= 1 and node::Buffer::HasInstance(info[0]); reject with a thrown JS error otherwise.
findings.failure_mode: Calling the method with no argument makes info[0] Undefined, so ->ToObject() throws/returns an empty handle; calling it with a non-Buffer object makes node::Buffer::Data/Length (lines 20-21) operate on an object that is not a Buffer. Either way ICU can be handed an invalid pointer/length pair, causing a crash or out-of-bounds read driven purely by caller input.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm no info.Length()/HasInstance guard exists before line 5 and observe behavior when the method is called with undefined or with a plain object/string.

findings.title: node::Buffer::Data/Length on unvalidated object passed to ucsdet_setText
findings.invariant: Data() and Length() must be applied only to a confirmed Node Buffer so that the pointer and length describe the same real, allocated region handed to ICU.
findings.failure_mode: When inputBuffer is not a genuine Buffer (consequence of the missing validation at line 5), node::Buffer::Data may return NULL or an unrelated pointer while node::Buffer::Length returns an unrelated size; ucsdet_setText then makes ICU read that many bytes from invalid memory, producing an out-of-bounds read or crash. This is the concrete sink of the validation gap and is listed distinctly because the sink is ICU's memory read, not the coercion itself.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm that inputBuffer is guaranteed to be a Buffer at this point; check what node::Buffer::Data returns for a non-Buffer object in the linked Node version.

findings.title: ToLocalChecked() aborts if charsetName is NULL
findings.bug_class: Process abort from checked-Maybe on NULL string construction
findings.invariant: charsetName must be verified non-NULL before constructing a V8 string, and a non-aborting conversion (or explicit empty check) should be used instead of blind .ToLocalChecked().
findings.failure_mode: If ucsdet_getName ever yields a NULL name (or the NULL-match bug is only half-fixed by checking errorCode but not the match pointer), Nan::New<v8::String>(NULL) produces an empty MaybeLocal and the chained .ToLocalChecked() intentionally aborts the process. It is a secondary crash site downstream of the same exceptional-condition mishandling.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm whether ucsdet_getName can return NULL for a valid match in this ICU version and whether charsetName is null-checked before line 56.

audit_checklist: Add an explicit 'if (charsetMatch == NULL)' failure path immediately after ucsdet_detect (line 31), before ucsdet_getName/ucsdet_getConfidence, independent of errorCode.
Reproduce the crash by calling DetectCharacterEncoding with an empty Buffer (Buffer.alloc(0)) and confirm ucsdet_detect returns NULL with errorCode == U_ZERO_ERROR.
Validate info.Length() >= 1 and node::Buffer::HasInstance(info[0]) before line 5; throw a clean TypeError otherwise.
Verify node::Buffer::Data/Length are only ever called on a confirmed Buffer so ICU never receives a mismatched pointer/length.
Null-check charsetName before Nan::New<v8::String>(charsetName).ToLocalChecked() to avoid a process abort.
Confirm ucsdet_close is reached on every path (it is in the shown code); ensure any added early returns also close the detector to avoid leaks.
